title: Severe Intraoperative Bronchospasm in an Asthmatic Patient
summary: A 28-year-old man with poorly controlled asthma develops severe bronchospasm shortly after intubation, when incision begins under a still-light anesthetic. Learners must read the shark-fin capnograph, exclude mechanical causes, deepen the anesthetic, escalate bronchodilators to epinephrine, and recognize breath-stacking hypotension that only a circuit disconnect will fix.
learningObjectives: Recognize bronchospasm from the prolonged-upslope (shark-fin) capnograph, rising peak airway pressures, and wheeze — and grade severity by the falling EtCO2 and SpO2 as the chest goes silent.
Exclude the mimics first: kinked or obstructed ETT, endobronchial intubation, circuit problems, aspiration, pneumothorax, and anaphylaxis.
Treat in escalating order: 100% oxygen with hand ventilation, deepen the anesthetic, albuterol 6-8 puffs via an in-line adapter, then IV epinephrine for severe or refractory spasm.
Ventilate an obstructed chest safely: slow rate, long expiratory time, and permissive hypercapnia to avoid dynamic hyperinflation.
Recognize breath-stacking (auto-PEEP) as a cause of hypotension and treat it by disconnecting the circuit to allow full exhalation.
Know the second-line agents — ipratropium, magnesium, ketamine, hydrocortisone — and the postoperative disposition of a patient after life-threatening bronchospasm.
setup: OR configured just after intubation for an open umbilical hernia repair: ETT secured, mechanical ventilation started, sevoflurane being introduced, surgeon prepping and eager to start.
Labeled props: albuterol MDI with in-line circuit adapter, ipratropium MDI, syringes of propofol, ketamine, epinephrine 10 mcg/mL and 100 mcg/mL dilutions, magnesium sulfate bag, hydrocortisone vial.
A suction catheter at the head of the bed so learners can concretely pass it down the ETT to exclude obstruction.
Confederate circulator who can report 'audible wheeze, almost no breath sounds now' when asked to auscultate late in the crisis.
Ventilator alarm cues: faculty should verbally report rising peak inspiratory pressures (e.g. '38... 44... 50 cmH2O') since the simulated monitor does not display airway pressure.
Preop note: poorly controlled asthma with an emergency visit for an exacerbation 3 months ago; used his albuterol inhaler in the preop bay this morning.

patient.name: Darnell Reed
pmh: Asthma since childhood; emergency department visit for exacerbation 3 months ago, never intubated
Symptomatic umbilical hernia scheduled for open repair
Frequent night cough and albuterol use in the past month — poorly controlled by GINA criteria

airway.notes: Grade 1 view; 8.0 ETT secured at 22 cm; intubated 2 minutes before scenario start with breath sounds initially equal.

patient.plan: Open umbilical hernia repair under general endotracheal anesthesia; sevoflurane maintenance being established at scenario start.

phases.label: Post-Intubation
phases.description: ETT just placed; anesthetic still light while sevoflurane washes in.

phases.label: Bronchospasm Onset
phases.description: Shark-fin capnograph, rising peak pressures, audible wheeze.

phases.label: Severe Bronchospasm
phases.description: Near-silent chest: EtCO2 barely registers, desaturation, hemodynamic strain.

phases.label: Resolution
phases.description: Airflow restored; capnograph normalizes; disposition planning.

events.label: Incision under light anesthesia
events.description: The surgeon begins while end-tidal sevoflurane is still low. Heart rate and pressure bump with stimulation — the airways of a poorly controlled asthmatic are primed to react.

events.label: Bronchospasm onset
events.description: The capnograph develops a prolonged upsloping (shark-fin) expiratory phase and EtCO2 climbs as emptying slows. Peak pressures rise (report '38... 44 cmH2O'), wheeze is audible, and SpO2 drifts down.

events.label: Severe bronchospasm — near-silent chest
events.description: Airflow nearly ceases: the capnograph trace shrinks (EtCO2 toward 18 — little gas is moving), SpO2 falls to the low 80s, and the blood pressure starts to sag as intrathoracic pressure climbs. Peak pressures 50+ cmH2O.

events.label: Breath-stacking hypotension (auto-PEEP)
events.description: Aggressive bagging without expiratory time stacks breaths: dynamic hyperinflation raises intrathoracic pressure, venous return collapses, and the pressure falls to 68/40. Fire if learners bag fast. The fix is counterintuitive — stop ventilating and open the circuit.

events.label: Circuit disconnect — trapped gas released
events.description: Disconnecting the circuit for 20-30 seconds lets the hyperinflated chest empty; venous return and blood pressure recover promptly. Resume ventilation slowly afterward.

events.label: Anesthetic deepened
events.description: Sevoflurane opened up (with a propofol bolus, or ketamine for its bronchodilation): spasm eases partially — EtCO2 recovers toward 26 and SpO2 to the high 80s, but the capnograph stays obstructed.

events.label: Albuterol 6-8 puffs via in-line adapter
events.description: First-line bronchodilator through the ETT (repeat as needed; much of each puff deposits in the circuit, so generous dosing is expected): wheeze loosens, EtCO2 toward 30, SpO2 to the low 90s.

events.label: Epinephrine IV response (severe/refractory spasm)
events.description: Titrated IV epinephrine (10-100 mcg boluses, roughly 1 mcg/kg, escalating to an infusion if needed) is the drug for life-threatening bronchospasm: airflow returns, SpO2 climbs, at the cost of tachycardia.

events.label: Ventilator adjusted for obstruction
events.description: Rate slowed to ~8/min with a long expiratory time (I:E 1:3 to 1:4), accepting a high CO2 (permissive hypercapnia) to avoid re-stacking breaths.

events.label: Second-line agents given
events.description: Ipratropium via the circuit, magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 20 minutes, ketamine 10-20 mg boluses, and hydrocortisone 200 mg IV (steroid benefit is delayed but starts now). Slow additional improvement.

events.label: Bronchospasm resolves
events.description: The capnograph squares off to a normal plateau, EtCO2 and SpO2 normalize, and peak pressures fall. The team discusses whether to proceed and where the patient recovers.

expectedActions.label: Recognize bronchospasm from the shark-fin capnograph, rising peak pressures, and wheeze
Hand-ventilate on 100% oxygen and exclude mimics: pass a suction catheter, check ETT depth, listen bilaterally
expectedActions.description: Kinked/obstructed ETT, endobronchial intubation, circuit failure, aspiration, and pneumothorax must be ruled out before committing to the bronchospasm pathway.

expectedActions.label: Deepen the anesthetic (volatile, propofol, or ketamine) — light anesthesia is the trigger
Give albuterol 6-8 puffs via an in-line adapter and repeat as needed
Actively consider anaphylaxis: check the blood pressure trend, look for rash/flushing, review recent drugs
expectedActions.description: Isolated bronchospasm with a maintained pressure early on argues against anaphylaxis — but say so explicitly.

expectedActions.label: Call for help when the chest goes quiet and ask the surgeon to pause
Slow the rate, lengthen expiration (I:E 1:3-1:4), and accept hypercapnia
Recognize breath-stacking hypotension and disconnect the circuit to let the chest empty
Give titrated IV epinephrine (10-100 mcg boluses) for severe or refractory bronchospasm
Add second-line agents: ipratropium, magnesium 2 g IV over 20 min, ketamine, hydrocortisone 200 mg IV
Update the surgical team and plan disposition: extended PACU or ICU observation, continued bronchodilators, and steroids

expectedProgression: Scenario opens two minutes after intubation with the anesthetic still light; incision starts around 2 minutes in.
By 3-4 minutes the capnograph develops a shark-fin upslope with climbing EtCO2 and reported peak pressures in the 40s — bronchospasm.
Learners should go to 100% oxygen, hand-ventilate to feel the compliance, exclude tube and circuit causes, deepen the anesthetic, and start albuterol.
By 6-7 minutes untreated spasm becomes severe: near-silent chest with EtCO2 in the teens and SpO2 in the low 80s — IV epinephrine territory.
If learners bag hard and fast, faculty fires breath-stacking hypotension; the circuit disconnect that fixes it is the scenario's signature counterintuitive move.
With deepening, albuterol, epinephrine, and an obstruction-friendly ventilator strategy, the spasm loosens over several minutes and the capnograph squares off.
The team then decides whether to proceed and commits to extended postoperative respiratory monitoring.
correctManagement: Switch to 100% oxygen and hand-ventilate to assess compliance directly.

Exclude the mimics systematically: pass a suction catheter down the ETT (kink/plug), check depth against the teeth (endobronchial), listen to both sides (pneumothorax), scan the circuit, and consider aspiration and anaphylaxis.
Ask the surgeon to pause stimulation and call for help early — severe bronchospasm can arrest.
Deepen the anesthetic: open the volatile (sevoflurane is a bronchodilator), or use propofol or ketamine boluses; avoid histamine-releasing drugs.
Give albuterol 6-8 puffs via an in-line MDI adapter and repeat — circuit deposition means generous dosing.
For severe or refractory spasm, give titrated IV epinephrine 10-100 mcg boluses (about 1 mcg/kg), escalating to an infusion if needed.
Ventilate for obstruction: slow rate, long expiratory time (I:E 1:3-1:4), and permissive hypercapnia rather than normal numbers.
If the pressure falls with a tight, hyperinflated chest, disconnect the circuit for 20-30 seconds to release trapped gas, then resume gently.
Add second-line agents: ipratropium via the circuit, magnesium sulfate 2 g IV over 20 minutes, ketamine, and hydrocortisone 200 mg IV.
Afterward, decide jointly whether to proceed, keep bronchodilators and steroids running, and arrange extended PACU or ICU respiratory monitoring.
commonErrors: Treating the ventilator instead of the patient: silencing the pressure alarm and turning the rate up, which stacks breaths and drops the blood pressure.
Skipping the mechanical checklist — a kinked tube or endobronchial intubation reads exactly like bronchospasm until a catheter and a depth check exclude it.
Leaving the anesthetic light while reaching for drugs: stimulation under light anesthesia is the driver, and deepening is the fastest treatment.
Giving 2 albuterol puffs and expecting an effect — most of each puff deposits in the circuit before reaching the bronchi.
Delaying IV epinephrine in a silent chest out of arrhythmia fear.
Chasing a normal EtCO2 with aggressive ventilation instead of accepting hypercapnia.
Missing anaphylaxis when hypotension joins bronchospasm — the two crises share treatment (epinephrine) but not workup and disposition.
Sending a patient who needed epinephrine for bronchospasm to routine day-surgery discharge.

points: The capnograph is the earliest objective bronchospasm monitor: a prolonged upslope appears before desaturation, and in severe spasm the EtCO2 falls because almost no gas moves — a low number can mean a worse chest, not a better one.
Bronchospasm under anesthesia is usually triggered by airway instrumentation or surgical stimulation under a light anesthetic in a susceptible patient; deepening is both treatment and prevention.
The mechanical differential (tube kink, plug, endobronchial intubation, circuit fault, pneumothorax, aspiration, anaphylaxis) must be actively excluded — no drug fixes a kinked tube.

Escalation ladder: 100% O2 and hand ventilation → deepen → albuterol via in-line adapter (6-8 puffs, repeated) → IV epinephrine 10-100 mcg titrated → infusion. Second line: ipratropium, magnesium 2 g IV, ketamine, hydrocortisone.
Ventilating an obstructed chest is about expiratory time: slow rate, I:E 1:3-1:4, permissive hypercapnia. A 'normal' minute ventilation is the enemy.

Dynamic hyperinflation (auto-PEEP) causes hypotension by strangling venous return; the treatment — disconnecting the circuit — feels wrong and saves the patient. It is also a differential for PEA in any obstructed, ventilated patient.

Epinephrine treats both severe bronchospasm and anaphylaxis, which buys time even when the diagnosis is uncertain — but the two diagnoses diverge in workup (tryptase, allergy referral) and must be distinguished afterward.
A patient who needed epinephrine for bronchospasm needs extended monitoring, continued bronchodilators, steroids, and an explicit handoff — not a routine discharge pathway.
questions: What was the first signal that this was bronchospasm, and how did the capnograph shape drive your diagnosis?
Which mechanical mimics did you exclude, and how — what did the suction catheter and depth check each rule out?
Why is deepening the anesthetic first-line treatment here? Which agents would you pick and which would you avoid?
When did IV epinephrine become the right drug, and what dose did you start with?
The blood pressure fell as the chest got tighter — walk through why, and why disconnecting the circuit fixed it.
What ventilator settings did you choose afterward, and what CO2 were you willing to accept?
How would you tell this apart from anaphylaxis, and what changes if you can't?
What does this patient need postoperatively, and what do you tell the next team?
